coredata.dc:title: Dual emission N-doped carbon dots as a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal probe for indigo carmine detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yecang
Dong, Xuemei
Wang, Minhui
Guo, Beibei
coredata.dc:description: Novel dual-emission fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using ascorbic acid and rhodamine B as precursors and melamine as nitrogen source. The obtained N-CDs exhibited dual-emitting peaks at 435 nm and 578 nm under the single excitation of 350 nm. The fluorescence at 578 nm was more effectively quenched by indigo carmine (IC) based on the internal filtration effect and aggregation-induced emission quenching. Meanwhile, the apparent color change of N-CDs from pink to blue-purple after adding various concentrations of IC could be clearly observed with the naked eye. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal probe based on N-CDs was developed for IC detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. The addition of IC caused the ratiometric fluorescent value (F 435/F 578) to increase linearly within the range from 0 to100 µM with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.18 µM and the colorimetric signal presented a linear response in the range of 0–133 µM with a LOD of 57.4 nM. Furthermore, the IC in juice drink, candy, and water was successfully detected. Besides, the N-CDs were also designed as a ratiometric temperature probe, and the ratiometric fluorescence signal (F 435/F 578) was linearly and reversibly responsive to temperature in the range of 20–75 °C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Indigo carmine
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of IC in real samples
Application of the probe in actual Samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N-CDs. Inset: the particle size distribution histogram and HRTEM image of N-CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs. (C) XPS-spectrum of N-CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (excitation wavelengths from 330 to 430 nm). Inset: photographs of N-CDs under visible light (left) and UV-light (right).
The influence of pH (A), NaCl concentration (B), UV irradiation time (C), and storage time (D) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the temperature range 20–75 °C. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 435/F 578) and the temperature, where F 435 and F 578 represent the fluorescence intensity at 435 nm and 578 nm, respectively. Inset: reversible fluorescence quenching and recovery of N-CDs with temperature switched between 20 and 75 °C.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs at different IC concentrations. (B) Linear relationship between F 435/F 578 and concentration of IC (0–100 µM). Inset: corresponding photographs of N-CDs under 365 nm UV light. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs at different IC concentrations. (D) Linear relationship between absorption intensity at 610 nm and concentration of IC (0–133 µM). Inset: corresponding photographs of N-CDs under daylight.

(A) and (B) The selectivity and interference studies of ratiometric fluorescent probe towards IC. Red bars: 60 μM individual interferents, black bars: a mixture of 60 μM IC with various interferents, where glutamic acid, benzoic acid, and ascorbic acid is 300 μM, and that of other is 600 μM. (C) The selectivity studies of colorimetric probe towards IC and (D) corresponding photographs of the solution under daylight. The concentration of IC is 60 μM and that of others is 600 μM.

(A) UV–visible spectra of CDs, IC, N-CDs + IC and fluorescence emission of N-CDs (excitation at 350 nm). (B) TEM of N-CDs + IC. Fluorescence decay curve of N-CDs in the absence and presence of IC at emission of 435 nm (C) and 578 nm (D).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs and the application diagram for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection of IC.
Detection results of IC in actual samples by fluorescent method.
Detection results of IC in actual samples by colorimetric method.

head.ce:title: Dual emission N-doped carbon dots as a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal probe for indigo carmine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-Based Materials, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science
The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-Based Materials, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intrinsic dual-emission fluorescent N-CDs were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CDs were implemented as a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal probe for IC detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. The N-CDs also used for temperature sensing by the ratiometric fluorescent method.

Novel dual-emission fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using ascorbic acid and rhodamine B as precursors and melamine as nitrogen source. The obtained N-CDs exhibited dual-emitting peaks at 435 nm and 578 nm under the single excitation of 350 nm. The fluorescence at 578 nm was more effectively quenched by indigo carmine (IC) based on the internal filtration effect and aggregation-induced emission quenching. Meanwhile, the apparent color change of N-CDs from pink to blue-purple after adding various concentrations of IC could be clearly observed with the naked eye. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal probe based on N-CDs was developed for IC detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. The addition of IC caused the ratiometric fluorescent value (F 435/F 578) to increase linearly within the range from 0 to100 µM with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.18 µM and the colorimetric signal presented a linear response in the range of 0–133 µM with a LOD of 57.4 nM. Furthermore, the IC in juice drink, candy, and water was successfully detected. Besides, the N-CDs were also designed as a ratiometric temperature probe, and the ratiometric fluorescence signal (F 435/F 578) was linearly and reversibly responsive to temperature in the range of 20–75 °C.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Indigo carmine is a synthetic food dye and extensively used in the food industry as a colorant to improve the appearance, color, and texture of food products. However, excessive or long-term consumption of food containing IC may endanger human health, cause toxicity to the liver, kidney, and central nervous system, and even lead to permanent corneal injury and cancer [1–3]. Therefore, for the sake of human health, the dosage of IC used in food has been strictly regulated in various countries [4,5]. According to Chinese food legislation, the maximum allowable level of IC in drinks is 0.1 g⋅kg−1 [6]. Given these facts, rapid and effective detection of IC concentration is of great significance for food safety. To date, some methods have been developed to analyze IC, mainly including high-performance liquid chromatography [4,5], UV–vis spectroscopy [7,8], and electrochemistry [1,9]. These methods have high accuracy and sensitivity, but they also have disadvantages such as specific instrumentation, complex pretreatment, and time-consuming. Fluorescence analysis has become a popular method for its facile operation, real-time detection, and high sensitivity. Nevertheless, to our knowledge, there is only one case of IC detection by fluorescence analysis [10].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) possess many excellent properties such as high quantum yield, adjustable fluorescence, low toxicity, and chemical inertness [11,12]. These features make CDs widely used in bioimaging, optical sensing, white light emission, photocatalysis, and other aspects [13–17]. However, most of the reported fluorescent CDs present single wavelength emission, and their fluorescence signals are easily affected by many external factors. Ratiometric fluorescent probes that display-two emission bands under a single excitation have been developed inthe pastfew years [18].

CDs-based dual-emitting probes are mainly obtained by integrating CDs with other fluorophores through physical or chemical methods [19]. For example, Bu et al.[20] designed a self-assembled complex of copper nanoclusters and CDs through electrostatic interaction, which was used as a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent probe for arginine and acetaminophen. Yan et al.[21] combined a blue zirconium MOF with red CDs as a dual-emission fluorescent probe for determining Cu2+. In the work reported by Kuang et al.[22], a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for assaying Cu2+ was constructed by covalently binding CDs with rhodamine B derivative via a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction. These double emission fluorescent probes can solve the fluctuation of fluorescent intensity utilizing self-calibration and show high sensitivity and selectivity for analytes[23]. However, the biggest drawback is that the synthesis step is too tedious and time-consuming. Thus, in recent years, some researchers prefer to develop a simple or even one-pot method to synthesize CDs with intrinsic dual-emitting properties[24–28]. Liu et al.[28] synthesized red/green dual-emission CDs by one-step synthesis, which were utilized for ONOO− sensing and cell imaging. A similar method was used to prepare blue/green dual-emissive CDs [29]. Nevertheless, to our knowledge, dual-emission CDs for assaying IC have not been reported.

In the present work, to prepare fluorescent carbon dots with dual emission, rhodamine B was selected in addition to ascorbic acid and melamine as precursors and a one-pot hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared N-CDs displayed an intrinsic dual-emission fluorescence at 435 and 578 nm under the excitation of 350 nm. The addition of IC quenched the fluorescence at 578 nm more effectively. Thus, the N-CDs were implemented as a ratiometric probe for IC quantitative analysis. Meanwhile, the presence of IC changed the color of the N-CDs solution from pink to blue-purple, which could make IC detection more visual and convincing. Therefore, a dual-mode probe based on ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry was developed and successfully applied to the detection of IC in actual samples.

Melamine and rhodamine B (RhB) were provided by Aladdin Reagent Co. ltd. Indigo carmine, ascorbic acid, fructose, galactose, maltose, glucose, glutamic acid, benzoic acid, trisodium citrate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, o-nitrophenol, m-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol, cysteine, glutathione, and other routine metal salts were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. These reagents were of analytical grade and used directly. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was used throughout the experiments.

The particlesize and morphology of the synthesized N-CDs wasprofiled with Tecnai G20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). A PE-983 fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer was used to measure the FTIR spectrum the N-CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were carried out on Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer and Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer, respectively. The absorption and emission spectra were measured on Hitachi U4100 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer and Hitachi F4700 spectrofluorometer, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime and absolute fluorescence quantum yield were determined by Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer. Zeta potential was recorded by using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The pH of solution was adjusted by the addition of 0.1 M HCl or NaOH and was measured with a digital pH acidity meter before experiments.

Typically, 0.126 g melamine, 0.176 g ascorbic acid, and 0.012 g RhB were successively dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and then reacted in a PTFE autoclave at 210 °C for 10 h. The resultant product was filtered with a 0.22 μm pore diameter membrane and further purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO of 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the N-CDs solution (1 mg⋅mL−1) was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator until use. For comparison, b-CDs were also prepared with ascorbic acid and melamine as the precursors under the same conditions.

For the detection of IC with N-CDs, 50 μL of N-CDs and various amounts of IC stock solution were mixed and diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. The mixture solution was vibrated at room temperature for 5 min and then measured by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrofluorometer. The selectivity of N-CDs toward IC was performed in the same way by adding potential interfering substances. All analysis experiments were carried out at room temperature unless otherwise specified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of IC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: River water, juice drink, and candy were selected to confirm the practicality of the N-CDs for analyzing IC. Tap water and river water were taken from our laboratory and Changjiang (Wuhu, China), respectively. The water samples were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. Juice drink and candy were obtained from the local market. The candy was dissolved in ultrapure water under ultrasound and further purified in the same way as the water sample. The juice drink was treated in a similar way. Then, 50 μL of N-CDs and a known amount of IC were spiked into the aforementioned filtrate for the recovery experiment.

In present work, ascorbic acid, melamineand, and Rh B are selected for the preparation of N-CDs through hydrothermal method. These precursors possess abundant functional groups such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups. They undergo condensation reaction to form chain compound intermediates, and are further polymerized, carbonized and passivated at high temperature to form N-CDs [17,30]. The morphology and size of the obtained N-CDs are analyzed by TEM. Fig. 1 A displays that the prepared N-CDs are spherical in shape and show good dispersion. The particle sizes are mostly within 1.0 to 3.5 nm and the average diameter is about 2.3 nm. A lattice spacing of 0.21 nm observed from the HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 1A) indicates that the N-CDs possess the (100) crystal plane structure of graphite carbon [31,32]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1A) displays a wide peak around 21.4°, revealing an amorphous carbon structure [28]. The surface functional groups and chemical composition of the N-CDs are revealed by FTIR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 1B, the broad peak centered at 3400 cm−1 is the overlap of O H and N H stretching vibration, and the small band in the range 3029–2856 cm−1 assigns to C H stretching vibration. The peaks located at 1722 and 1625 cm−1 account for the stretching vibration of CO and CN [21], respectively, while the peaks at 1410 and 1096 cm−1 are assigned to C N heterocycle [33] and C O C stretching vibration. From these data, we can infer that the N-CDs are functionalized by hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups. In addition, CC stretching vibration at 1451 cm−1 and CH out-of-plane bending vibration from 698 to 758 cm−1 are observed, which are the characteristic absorption of the aromatic ring in RhB molecular structure [34], indicating that there is RhB residues on the surface of N-CDs. Fig. 1C presents three peaks at 284.52, 399.46, and 531.91 eV, which are respectively attributed to the characteristic peak of C1s, N1s, and O1s. The corresponding ratio of three elements are 72.70 %, 6.70 %, and 21.00 %. In the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S1B), three peaks at 284.81, 286.35, and 288.34 eV are corresponded to CC/C C, C N/C O, and CO/CN. The peaks at 399.72 and 401.66 eV in the high-resolution N1s spectrum shown in Fig. S1C are attributed to C N C and N H, respectively. From the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. S1D) two peaks at 531.4 and 532.48 eV are observed, belonging to CO and C O C/C − OH, respectively. The functional groups identified by XPS spectra are consistent with the FTIR results, further demonstrating that the N-CDs are rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups, which can improve the solubility of N-CDs in water [35].

The UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 1D displays two absorption bands. The band centered at 250 is assigned to the π-π* electron transition of the CC group [10], and the other peak at 554 nm is similar to the characteristic absorption of RhB, which supports the FTIR result and further proves the presence of RhB moiety on the N-CDs surface. The fluorescence spectra exhibit two emission bands of blue and orange and the N-CDs solution emits orange fluorescence under UV light (Inset in Fig. 1D). After carefully analyzing the diagram, it is found that as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 430 nm, the blue fluorescence peak at 419 nm is red shifted to 499 nm with the emission intensity ascending first and then descending, while the orange emission located at 578 nm presents excitation-independent fluorescence behavior. Besides, under the excitation of 350 nm wavelength, the fluorescence intensity of the two peaks at 435 and 578 nm reaches the maximum and the absolute quantum yield of N-CDs is 4.9 %. Previous research pointed out that the intrinsic double emission fluorescence of carbon dots stems from carbon core and surface organic fluorophores [26,36]. To gain insight into the dual-emission phenomenon, b-CDs were prepared only with ascorbic acid and melamine (without RhB) under the identical reaction condition. As depicted in Fig. S2, only one ultraviolet absorption band at 250 nm and a single blue fluorescence signal possessing excitation-dependent emission are observed. In addition, it has been found that free RhB molecule exhibits excitation-independent emission at ca. 580 nm [37]. Based on the above analysis, we believe that the blue emission of N-CDs showing excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior may originate from the carbon core that is formed by pyrolysis of ascorbic acid and melamine, while the orange fluorescence showing excitation-independence is emitted from RhB that is integrated with the carbon core via amidation reaction. Such N-CDs with dual-emitting properties could be good candidates for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing.

As we all know, the stability is essential for N-CDs as a fluorescent probe, so the fluorescence performances under various conditions are evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2
A, when pH value increases from 2 to 12, the emission intensity at 435 nm is almost unchanged (A similar result is observed for b-CDs in Fig. S3A), whereas the intensity at 578 nm gradually increases. This enhancement might be attributed to the change of surface charge induced by the protonation-deprotonation of the carboxyl and amino groups on the N-CDs surface [38,39]. This variation is evidenced by a decrease in the Zeta potential from + 2.02 mV to 12.03 mV (Fig. S4). From Fig. 2B, we can find that the fluorescence intensities for both blue and orange emission have little changed even under the concentration of NaCl as high as 1000 mM in the solution, signifying excellent salt tolerance. Similar results are obtained in terms of the influences of UV irradiation time (Fig. 2C). Even after 2000 s of continuous irradiation under UV light, the change in fluorescence intensity is almost negligible, suggesting that N-CDs have a charming photostability. Besides, Fig. 2D presents that after 6 weeks of storage at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity changes less than 20 %, indicating good storage stability. The above results suggest that the N-CDs possess brilliant fluorescence stability, which makes them prospecting for practical applications.

The effect of temperature on the N-CDs is shown in Fig. 3A. The fluorescence signal at 435 nm preserves unchanged in a wide range of temperatures raising. A similar result can be observed for the b-CDs sample (Fig. S3B). But decreased fluorescence intensity at 578 nm is displayed with an increase of temperature. This temperature dependent behavior is attributed to the RhB moiety on N-CDs. As we know, the fluorescence of RhB and its derivatives is highly sensitivity to temperature, which is due to the Boltzmann distribution of electrons within the excited state level and the dependence on temperature is a function of the specific vibronic band structure of the material. Thermal energy will excite the electrons within the excited state to the different vibrational states, which are overlapping at different energy levels [40,41]. Therefore, nonradiative transitions become possible at higher temperatures, resulting diminished luminescence intensity. Interestingly, the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F 435/F 578) increases linearly with temperature from 20 to 75 °C, which satisfies a linear equation of F 435/F 578 = 0.4649 + 0.0076 T/oC with correlation coefficients (R2 ) of 0.990 (Fig. 3B). This remarkable temperature-response feature makes the N-CDs a promising ratiometric temperature probe. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 3B, the F 435/F 578 value remains unchanged as the temperature is switched between 20 and 75 °C after six heating/cooling cycles, suggesting that the N-CDs have good stability for temperature sensing.

Due to their dual emission characteristics, N-CDs are promising to be designed as a ratiometric fluorescent probe. As expected, the addition of IC quenches the N-CDs fluorescence obviously. To ensure that the designed probe is under optimal conditions for IC sense, the effects of response time and pH value are further tested. As displayed in Fig. S5A, the highest fluorescence quenching appears at pH 3. However, considering the simplicity of the experiment, the pH of the solution is fixed at pH 7 in the subsequent work. As for the response time, Fig. S5B shows that the fluorescence quenchingis quite rapid after adding IC and achieves stabilization within 1 min, so the incubation time for IC detection is 5 min.

Under the optimal conditions, the response of the fluorescent N-CDs toward IC is illustrated in Fig. 4
A. With the increase of IC concentration, both the blue and orange emission intensities decrease progressively, while the quenching of the latter is more obvious. Also, the fluorescence color markedly changed from orange to light blue alongside with the increase of IC concentration (Inset of Fig. 4B) can be effectively recognized by naked eyes. Fig. 4B reveals that the value of F 435/F 578 displays a linear increase with IC concentration in the range of 0–100 μM, and a regression equation is F 435/F 578 = 0.0178[C IC/μM] + 0.7508. The detection limit (LOD) is 0.18 μM. Simultaneously, the colorimetric signal presents a significant response to IC. From the UV–vis absorption spectra of Fig. 4C, we can find that a new absorption peak at 610 nm appears and its absorbance increases alongside the IC concentration. Moreover, the color of the N-CDs solution under visible light apparently varies from pink to blue-purple (Inset of Fig. 4D). A good linear relation between absorbance value and IC concentration is obtained (Fig. 4D). The linear regression equation is Abs = 0.0085[C IC/μM] + 0.0689 in the concentration rang of 0–133 µM with R 2 = 0.995 and the LOD of 0.057 μM.

The experimental results of the proposed method are compared with those of previously reported methods in monitoring IC. As is obvious in Table S1, our dual-signal detection method provides much wider concentration ranges and acceptable lower LODs. More specifically, no matter under ultraviolet light or sunlight, the distinct color change of N-CDs with the gradual addition of IC can be easily recognized with the bare eye [21,42]. Therefore, these results demonstrate that N-CDs can be designed as a sensitive probe for detecting IC by both ratiometric fluorometric and colorimetric methods.

High selectivity and good anti-interference ability are the critical properties for fluorescent probes in practical applications. Therefore, the fluorescence responses of N-CDs to some common interfering ions and organic molecules are investigated. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, except for IC, the fluorescence of N-CDs is not significantly affected by other interferents at the same concentration. Meanwhile, when each interfering substance coexists with IC, insignificant fluorescence change is observed. In addition, the same results can be obtained from the colorimetric method (Fig. 5C and D). It can be seen that except for IC, only TNP causes a new absorption peak at 400 nm, while the color of the N-CDs solution in the presence of TNP is still pink. The results illustrate that both ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry reveal fantastic selectivity and good anti-interference in IC detection.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by IC can be understood from several spectroscopic measurements. Fig. 6 A displays that the multiple wide absorption bands of IC at 200–400 nm and 500–700 nm obviously overlap with the excitation wavelength (350 nm) and the orange emission peak (578 nm) of N-CDs, respectively, which enables the generation of the inner filter effect (IFE) between the N-CDs and IC [43,44]. To confirm this inference, we measured the fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs and the results are shown in Fig. 6C and D. Clearly, the fluorescence lifetimes both at 435 and 578 nm for N-CDs with and without IC have almost no change, demonstrating that the IC quenches the fluorescence of N-CDs primarily through the IFE effect. Moreover, new aggregates with an average diameter of 5.7 nm are obtained upon the addition of IC (Fig. 6B), which is larger than that of bare N-CDs, inferring that IC leads to the aggregation of carbon dots. Further characterization shows that the zeta potentials for N-CDs, IC, and the mixture (N-CDs + IC) are − 5.99, −7.01, and − 13.70 mV, respectively. These results rule out the electrostatic attraction between N-CDs and IC, while other interactions such as π–π interaction and hydrogen bond may be responsible for the formation of aggregates. According to the structure of the N-CDs and IC molecules, the π–π interaction can be established due to the benzene ring in the N-CDs structure and IC molecules. In addition, multi-hydrogen bonds such as O H N and O H O can also be formed from carboxylic, amino, and sulfonate groups presented on the N-CDs and IC[45]. Therefore, the addition of IC causes the aggregation of adjacent N-CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Based on these analyses, the quenching mechanism of N-CDs by IC mainly derives from IFE and aggregation-induced emission quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the probe in actual Samples.
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to further test the practicability of the designed probe, water, juice drink, and candy are selected as real samples for analyzing IC. The concentration of IC is determined by the standard addition method, and the results are presented in Tables 1 and 2
. For the ratiometric fluorescent method, the recoveries in these samples are from 98.73 to 102.70 % with RSD less than 2.33 %. Similarly, their recoveries by the colorimetric method are found to be 98.63–100.78 % with the RSD less than 2.17 %. The results reveal that the dual-signal probe has excellent accuracy for IC detection in actual samples.

In summary, intrinsic dual-emission fluorescent N-CDs were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-fabricated N-CDs can be designed as a ratiometric fluorescent temperature probe with remarkable sensitivity and reversibility. Moreover, the N-CDs have been implemented as a ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-signal probe for selective and sensitive detection of IC. Further studies demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by IC is the synergistic effect of IFE and aggregation-induced emission quenching. The effectiveness of this dual-signal method is verified by assaying IC in river water, juice drink, and candy.

Yecang Tang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xuemei Dong: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Minhui Wang: Investigation, Validation. Beibei Guo: Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122310.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical determination of indigo carmine in food and water samples using a novel platform based on chiral amine-bis(phenolate) boron complex
Dyes Pigm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Incebay, A. Kilic, Electrochemical determination of indigo carmine in food and water samples using a novel platform based on chiral amine-bis(phenolate) boron complex, Dyes and Pigments, 197 (2022) 109921.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization and application of alkali-soluble polysaccharide of Carica papaya seeds for removal of indigo carmine and Congo red dyes from single and binary solutions
J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.K. Fatombi, S.A. Osseni, E.A. Idohou, I. Agani, D. Neumeyer, M. Verelst, R. Mauricot, T. Aminou, Characterization and application of alkali-soluble polysaccharide of Carica papaya seeds for removal of indigo carmine and Congo red dyes from single and binary solutions, Journal of Environmental Chemical Engineering, 7 (2019) 103343.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current treatment technologies and mechanisms for removal of indigo carmine dyes from wastewater: A review
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.F. Chowdhury, S. Khandaker, F. Sarker, A. Islam, M.T. Rahman, M.R. Awual, Current treatment technologies and mechanisms for removal of indigo carmine dyes from wastewater: A review, Journal of Molecular Liquids, 318 (2020) 114061.

Eur. Food Res. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Kirschbaum, C. Krause, H. Brückner, Liquid chromatographic quantification of synthetic colorants in fish roe and caviar, European Food Research and Technology, 222 (2005) 572-579.

Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mathiyalagan, B.K. Mandal, Y.C. Ling, Determination of synthetic and natural colorants in selected green colored foodstuffs through reverse phase-high performance liquid chromatography, Food chemistry, 278 (2019) 381-387.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and rapid resonance Rayleigh scattering method for detection of indigo carmine in soft drink
Luminescence

Part A, Molecular and biomolecular spectroscopy
